instruction: Take the leftmost door. Walk past the large painting to your left. Take a left and wait in the doorway.

text: Let me start by taking a closer look at what we're working with.
I should look around and explore what doors or options are available in this space.
I'm scanning the perimeter of this large empty hall, rotating to get a complete view of the space and look for any exits.
I'm noticing curtained alcoves that might be concealing doorways, so I should continue exploring to locate actual doors.

We're cycling through a full 360-degree rotation—starting from the original view, then the hall at 90 degrees, curtains at 180, and this cabinet view at 270. One more set of turns completes the circle back to the start. Still, none of these perspectives reveal an obvious leftmost door.

Let me rotate back to the original position and examine it more carefully for doors on the left side. The first image had some features I should look at more closely—there's a dark opening or furniture piece on the left and an arch with balustrade on the right. I'll complete the full rotation by turning left 6 more times to return to the starting orientation, then systematically check each view for any doors I might have missed.

I'm back at the starting position and now I need to systematically explore by turning left in smaller increments to locate any doors. Looking at the layout more carefully, I can see an ornate stone archway with decorative carving on the right side that could be a doorway, while the left side has dark wooden furniture and a mirror, though there appears to be a darker archway partially visible further left that might be worth investigating.

Let me turn left slightly to get a better view of the left wall.

I notice there's a curtained alcove on the left that's probably concealing a doorway, so I'll keep moving left to explore further.

Looking at this 180-degree view, I'm noticing three curtained alcoves that appear to be windows rather than doors, based on the furniture arrangement below them—there's bed-like furniture positioned underneath, which suggests this might be a bedroom. Let me continue examining the space.
I notice there's a darker doorway on the right side next to the curtained alcove—an actual opening rather than just fabric. I should keep scanning the space to locate additional doors.

I'm recalculating the rotation: 42 turns at 15 degrees each gives 630 degrees total, which is 1.75 full circles rather than the single rotation I expected. Let me verify this against my step-by-step count to see where the discrepancy is. I need to backtrack from my current position at 270 degrees by turning right 3 times to return to that 225-degree angle where I spotted the dark passage.

I'm looking at a 225-degree view and spotted a dark doorway to the right of the curtained bed alcove that appears to open into another room. I should rotate the view a bit more to center on that opening.
I'm noticing the fireplace on the left side of the room, and there's more space to explore. I should check to the right where I spotted that dark doorway earlier.

I'm not seeing an obvious exit in this enclosed space—maybe what I thought was the leftmost door is actually that archway with the balustrade from the first image, or possibly the darker opening I glimpsed at the edge of the cabinet view. Let me take another look at that 270-degree angle since it showed a hint of a darker archway.

Actually, the light-stone archway with balustrade in the very first image looks like a real doorway with steps and railing leading out. Let me navigate back to that starting orientation and examine the right side more carefully. Since I can't strafe, I'll need to rotate and move forward strategically.

I'll do a systematic 360-degree scan, turning 15 degrees at a time and observing after each rotation to map out all the doors precisely—that's about 24 steps, which is manageable. Let me reset my orientation first and work through this methodically.

I'm realizing this first image is actually close to the starting position, just with a slightly different angle that's making the dining chairs more prominent in the foreground. Rather than trying to pinpoint the exact degree, I should systematically scan every 15 degrees with a single rotation at each position to map out the space consistently.

I'm reconsidering the room layout and wondering if that arched doorway with the balustrade from the first image might actually be the leftmost entrance I'm looking for—it's a distinct stone-framed opening rather than just a wall mirror. Let me step back and carefully map out the left and right sides of this space again.

I'm verifying that the turning mechanics are consistent with the 50-turn view, so I'll do a systematic reset and rotate left through a full 360-degree revolution, documenting each step to map out the exact degree positions. Actually, rather than spending time on that detailed rotation mapping, I should focus on finding doors by moving forward toward the walls where openings are most likely to be—especially since the initial instruction mentions taking the leftmost door first.

Let me go back to the true starting position and do a complete panoramic scan with regular intervals to identify all visible doors, then select the leftmost one. I'll start with larger rotation increments to cover the full 360 degrees efficiently, then zoom in on the areas where doors appear.
The 180-degree view is matching up with the three curtain alcoves, so my tracking seems consistent. I'm going to continue scanning visually in 30-degree increments to map out the rest of the space.

I'm noticing a tall, light-colored panel on the far left edge of the frame that looks like a door or doorway with a darker opening. I should pan the view leftward to get a better look at it.

I notice there's a doorway in the center-left area of the image—a dark opening between two paintings with a clock visible on the wall nearby. I should move forward and turn left slightly to get a better view of this actual door opening.

We've completed a full rotation back to the starting position, which confirms the scan covered the entire room. The only viable door candidate is that dark opening between the paintings with the clock nearby, spotted a couple of steps back in the sequence.

I can see the doorway now—it's the dark opening between the two paintings in the center-left area, right next to the clock. I need to adjust my view by turning left slightly to center it better before moving forward.
The door is now properly positioned in the center of the wall, framed by ornate mirrors on either side with a clock mounted to the left. I should head toward it.
I can see the doorway now—a dark rectangular opening between the wall panel and the painting. I need to adjust my heading and move forward toward it.

I'm getting a clearer view of the wooden door set in an ornate stone frame with a dark gap showing it's slightly ajar. Before I conclude this is the leftmost door, I should check if there are other doors further to the left along the wall.

I'm scanning the full wall now—there's the wooden door in the center slightly ajar, an ornate stone structure like a fireplace or niche to its left with a painting above it, and a bench further left. I should check if there are more doors extending leftward or on the right side of where I was looking.

I'm trying to map out the spatial layout of this room more carefully. I'm getting a fuller picture of the room layout now—the dining table is visible in the center, with the curtain alcove on the left side and the wooden door frame on the right, so the door is positioned to the right of the curtains. I want to continue panning left to see what's beyond those curtains.
I'm getting a better view of the space now with the curtains and dining chairs visible, so I'll continue rotating left to see more of the room.

I'm mapping out the room layout—it appears to be one large space with a wooden door I've already found, a dining area, three curtained alcoves that are likely windows, a cabinet, and a fireplace with mirror. Since I don't see another actual door besides the wooden one, the "leftmost door" probably refers to that wooden door being positioned on the near wall, possibly alongside an archway with a balustrade from my original vantage point that might function as another entrance on an adjacent wall.

Let me reconsider that stone archway from the first image—it could be a decorative mirror or panel rather than a door, similar to other ornate stone frames I've seen that turned out to be mirrors. Since the wooden door is the only one showing clear depth and darkness indicating an actual passage, I should go through it. But first, let me complete a full scan of the remaining walls I haven't fully checked—the wall opposite where I started and the wall with the fireplace—to confirm I've seen everything before proceeding.

The ornate stone panels are definitely mirrors rather than doors, which confirms what I suspected. I'm nearly done with my full rotation around the space and should be approaching my starting position soon.

I'm spotting a wooden door on the left side of the frame—dark wood paneling with an open entrance revealing a shadowy interior. I should rotate my view leftward to get a better look and see if this is the same door from before or a different one.

I've confirmed this wooden door is the same one I spotted earlier, so there's only one real door on this wall section. After nearly completing a full rotation—I've passed the dining area, multiple curtain alcoves, cabinet and mirror sections, a fireplace mirror, and now back to this door—it's clear this is the only exit, making it the leftmost door by default.

Now I'll approach it and go through.
Approaching the door now, I'll adjust my heading to aim directly for it.
The door's positioned in the center now and looks slightly open with darkness visible inside. I should push the scene forward.
I'm getting closer to the double-door entrance and can see it's made up panels. Let me position myself directly in front of it to get a better look and figure out how to open it.

Looking at the double doors more carefully, they appear mostly closed, though there might be a slight gap suggesting one panel is partially open. I should move closer to get a better look at whether there's actually an opening or if this is just a sealed double door I need to proceed through anyway.
